1. Label TEE-capable nodes with the Node: 1. Label TEE-capable nodes with the Node Feature Discovery operator.
2. Enable confidential containers and cr: 2. Enable confidential containers and create a KataConfig to install the kata-cc runtime.
3. Build initdata and deploy a pod with: 3. Build initdata and deploy a pod with runtimeClassName: kata-cc, then verify its attestation from the Workloads list.
A confidential pod attests to Trustee on: A confidential pod attests to Trustee on boot. Check the result from the Confidential Data Hub inside the guest — run this against the pod and expect the response "success":
A KBS resource the guest fetches as proo: A KBS resource the guest fetches as proof it attested — released only after a successful attestation. Use the full path <repository>/<name>/<key>, e.g. default/kbsres1/key1. A two-segment path like default/kbsres1 is a folder, not a resource, and returns 404.
A non-success response (or a connection: A non-success response (or a connection error) means the guest could not reach Trustee or its TEE evidence was rejected. The attestation-status resource must be configured in Trustee for this check to return a value.
A Trustee-delivered passphrase reference: A Trustee-delivered passphrase reference like kbs:///default/luks/passphrase, or a Kubernetes Secret name.
Add attestation evidence sidecar (self-r: Add attestation evidence sidecar (self-reporting, no exec required)
Adds runAsNonRoot: true so the pod meets: Adds runAsNonRoot: true so the pod meets the restricted Pod Security Standard (allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, dropped capabilities, and the RuntimeDefault seccomp profile are always set). Untick only if your image must run as root.
AMD SEV-SNP — the attestation report is: AMD SEV-SNP — the attestation report is generated in-guest; ensure the Trustee SNP verifier can fetch VCEK collateral from the AMD KDS.
An IOMMU MachineConfig (intel_iommu=on /: An IOMMU MachineConfig (intel_iommu=on / amd_iommu=on) — reboots nodes.
Applying this MachineConfig rolls out a: Applying this MachineConfig rolls out a reboot to every node in the "{{role}}" pool, one at a time.
Attach a raw-block PVC that an init cont: Attach a raw-block PVC that an init container opens with LUKS inside the TEE, using a passphrase Trustee delivers only after attestation.
Attestation needs the host quote-generat: Attestation needs the host quote-generation stack for your TEE. Without it the guest sends an empty quote and Trustee rejects it — pods still run, but no secret is ever released. Confirm it is deployed for your hardware:
Available once the kata-cc runtime is in: Available once the kata-cc runtime is installed on your TEE nodes.
Confidential containers are enabled, so: Confidential containers are enabled, so the operator installs the kata-cc runtime.
Confidential containers is enabled clust: Confidential containers is enabled cluster-wide. Pick the TEE-capable nodes you intend to run confidential workloads on — placement is per-workload, via the runtime class and a node selector. The snippet below updates as you choose.
Confidential containers run your workloa: Confidential containers run your workload inside a hardware Trusted Execution Environment (Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP), so data stays encrypted in use — even from the cluster host. Work through the checklist below to go from a fresh cluster to an attested confidential workload.
Confidential containers run your workload inside a hardware Trusted Execution Environment (Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP), so data stays encrypted in use — even from the host. To get started:
Confidential workloads use a kata-cc run: Confidential workloads use a kata-cc runtime class and run inside a hardware TEE. Deploy one with runtimeClassName: kata-cc and initdata to see it here.
Could not enable Intel TDX host: Could not enable Intel TDX host
Create a KataConfig with confidential co: Create a KataConfig with confidential containers enabled to install the kata-cc runtime on your TEE nodes. This reboots the selected nodes.
Delete {{kind}} {{name}} in {{namespace}: Delete {{kind}} {{name}} in {{namespace}}? Its confidential VM will be torn down.
Deploy a workload with runtimeClassName:: Deploy a workload with runtimeClassName: kata-cc, then use the Verify attestation action on the Workloads list to check it.
Detect and label your Intel TDX or AMD S: Detect and label your Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP nodes with Node Feature Discovery. If a node has TDX enabled in firmware but is not detected, activate the TDX host kernel arguments first. Confidential workloads only schedule onto these nodes.
Enable confidential containers in the Op: Enable confidential containers in the OpenShift sandboxed containers operator — a supported configuration option. The operator then installs the kata-cc runtime on your TEE nodes.
Enable TEE detection and the Intel TDX h: Enable TEE detection and the Intel TDX host first. The snippet below targets any TDX node by label.
If the error mentions a missing resource: If the error mentions a missing resource, the Node Feature Discovery operator is probably not installed.
Image whose init container opens the LUK: Image whose init container opens the LUKS device with the passphrase on boot — see the OpenShift sandboxed containers LUKS-in-TEE docs.
Installing the kata-cc runtime reconfigu: Installing the kata-cc runtime reconfigures and reboots the sandboxed-containers nodes, one at a time.
Installing the runtime reconfigures and: Installing the runtime reconfigures and reboots the eligible nodes, one at a time. checkNodeEligibility limits installation to nodes labeled TEE-capable.
Intel TDX — deploy a Quote Generation Se: Intel TDX — deploy a Quote Generation Service (QGS) and a PCCS for PCK collateral (Intel DCAP). The guest reaches the QGS over vsock.
Intel TDX must be activated in the host: Intel TDX must be activated in the host kernel before Node Feature Discovery can detect it. This adds the required kernel arguments to the selected MachineConfigPool. Use it when a node has TDX enabled in firmware but is not detected as TEE-capable.
It uses a tiny ubi-minimal image and pus: It uses a tiny ubi-minimal image and pushes the evidence to the Kubernetes API with curl (no oc, no python), so it fits inside the confidential guest VM.
Match the quote-generation stack to your: Match the quote-generation stack to your TEE — Intel TDX: QGS + PCCS; AMD SEV-SNP: AMD KDS; NVIDIA GPU: NRAS.
Name: Name
Namespace {{name}} does not exist yet an: Namespace {{name}} does not exist yet and will be created.
No nodes are labeled with a Trusted Exec: No nodes are labeled with a Trusted Execution Environment yet. Use Enable TEE detection to set up Node Feature Discovery and label Intel TDX or AMD SEV-SNP nodes.
No results match the current filters: No results match the current filters
No TEE-capable nodes detected. Install t: No TEE-capable nodes detected. Install the Node Feature Discovery operator and a NodeFeatureRule to label TDX / SEV-SNP nodes.
Nodes are labeled by the Node Feature Di: Nodes are labeled by the Node Feature Discovery operator via a NodeFeatureRule: intel.feature.node.kubernetes.io/tdx and amd.feature.node.kubernetes.io/snp mark TEE hardware; nvidia.com/cc.mode.state and nvidia.com/cc.ready.state mark confidential-computing GPUs.
Nodes labeled nvidia.com/cc.mode.state=o: Nodes labeled nvidia.com/cc.mode.state=on, nvidia.com/cc.ready.state=true, and a TEE label.
NVIDIA GPU — confidential GPU attestatio: NVIDIA GPU — confidential GPU attestation uses NVIDIA NRAS; ensure the Trustee NVIDIA GPU verifier is configured.
One will be created so NFD actually scan: One will be created so NFD actually scans your nodes. This needs the Node Feature Discovery operator — if creating fails, install it from OperatorHub first.
Paste the initdata your Trustee admin sh: Paste the initdata your Trustee admin shared with you — the cc_init_data value they generated on the Trustee, whether it runs in this cluster or a remote one. Without it the workload cannot attest.
Run a declared container inside the TEE: Run a declared container inside the TEE that continuously fetches a KBS resource to prove attestation and publishes a timestamped evidence record the Trustee plugin reads.
The kata-cc runtime class is created whe: The kata-cc runtime class is created when confidential containers are enabled and a KataConfig is installed on TEE-capable nodes.
The kata-cc-nvidia-gpu runtime needs the: The kata-cc-nvidia-gpu runtime needs the GPU stack enabled on your TEE nodes first (NVIDIA H100, bare metal only):
The NVIDIA GPU Operator with a ClusterPo: The NVIDIA GPU Operator with a ClusterPolicy enabling ccManager (CC mode on), the kata sandbox device plugin, and vfio-manager.
The role label of the MachineConfigPool: The role label of the MachineConfigPool holding your TEE nodes — for example "worker", or a custom pool such as "kata-oc".
The sidecar publishes that record as a C: The sidecar publishes that record as a ConfigMap the Trustee plugin reads in its Attestation status view. It must be present at pod creation; it cannot be added to a running pod.
The sidecar runs inside the TEE as a dec: The sidecar runs inside the TEE as a declared container — not via oc exec, which secure confidential workloads forbid. It proves attestation by fetching a KBS resource through the Confidential Data Hub (the resource is only released after a successful attestation) and pushes a timestamped evidence record to a ConfigMap.
This creates a KataConfig that installs: This creates a KataConfig that installs the kata-cc runtime on your TEE-capable nodes. Enable confidential containers first — the operator builds the kata-cc runtime from that.
This enables confidential containers in: This enables confidential containers in the OpenShift sandboxed containers operator — a supported configuration option. The operator then installs the kata-cc runtime on your TEE nodes.
This labels nodes that have a Trusted Ex: This labels nodes that have a Trusted Execution Environment (Intel TDX, AMD SEV-SNP) so confidential workloads can be scheduled on them. The Node Feature Discovery operator does the scanning.
